name: surgeon_ar_veteran_training_patient_risk
premise: Chief of surgery Alex Alvarez must decide whether to schedule complex spinal cases for residents training on a new AR navigation system that shortens operations by sixteen minutes on average. Device rep and veteran surgeon Bram Santos warns that AR misregisters on patients with atypical anatomy roughly three percent of the time, with no bedside fix yet. Veterans Affairs routing agreements send more complex veteran spines this quarter; denying AR for them preserves safety but slows the queue, breaching wait-time agreements with Congressional overseers.

agents.name: Alex Alvarez
agents.identity: I am the chief of surgery responsible for resident education, patient outcomes, and throughput agreements with referring hospitals.
agents.goal: I want innovation adopted without turning high-complexity veterans into unconsented training subjects for imperfect AR.

agents.name: Bram Santos
agents.identity: I am a senior attending who proctors AR cases and documents misregistration near nerve roots when anatomy deviates from atlas norms.
agents.goal: I want AR limited to cases within validated envelopes until patch releases ship, even if that slows the OR slate.

choices: I will allow AR for all scheduled cases with attending takeover clauses, accepting that overnight fatigue may still miss misregistration cues.
I will restrict AR to standard-anatomy cases this quarter and move complex veteran spines to non-AR slots, accepting wait-time penalties and political heat.
forced_choice.call_to_action: The OR block starts at six a.m. and scheduling must freeze by midnight. Alex Alvarez must choose between unrestricted AR rollout or restricted use for complex cases. What does Alex Alvarez do?
